data.title: Peruvian stocks, sol recoup some losses; focus on Middle East talks
data.description:
Key points:
  • Peruvian assets rebound after election-driven selloff, uncertainty persists
  • Brazil raises 5 billion euros in bonds; companies announce new debt sales
  • Brazil's economic activity index beats forecasts in March
  • Petrobras and Ecopetrol shares gain as crude prices rise 2%

By Johann M Cherian

Peruvian stocks and the sol rose on Thursday, steadying after a sharp selloff in the previous session on election uncertainties, while caution prevailed across Latin America as investors awaited more clarity on potential talks to end the Middle East conflict.

MSCI's index tracking equities in the copper producer (.MIPE00000PUS) edged up 2.7%, recouping some of its losses on Wednesday and a U.S.-listed exchange traded fund (.EPU) rose 1.8%, while the sol inched up 0.2% after the previous session's 1.6% slide.

However, international bonds were marginally lower on Thursday, with those maturing in 2060 down about 0.4 cents on the dollar, reflecting investor nervousness that left-wing congressman Roberto Sanchez was narrowing his gap with market-friendly frontrunner Keiko Fujimori in a tight presidential race. A runoff is scheduled for June 7.

Sanchez, a former cabinet minister under ousted leftist ex-President Pedro Castillo, has supported stronger state control over natural resources, proposed reviewing mining and gas contracts, taxing windfall profits and imposing a wealth tax.

"Sanchez has indicated that he won't be as market friendly in terms of policy ideas, so overall, that situation is (fueling uncertainty) in terms of what will happen to the central bank and checks and balances of the economy," said Andrés Abadía, chief Latam economist at Pantheon Macroeconomics.

The possibility of a less-market friendly candidate at the helm of the country weighed on sentiment on Wednesday. The share index fell over 7%, marking its biggest daily drop since July 2021 - the month when Castillo was elected as the country's president.

MIDEAST JITTERS PREVAIL

Following several sessions of gains on the back of potential talks to end the Middle East conflict, investors were taking a pause. MSCI's index tracking Latam stocks (.MILA00000PUS) slipped 0.4%, while the currencies benchmark (.MILA00000CUS) dipped 0.2% against the dollar.

Both the indexes have recouped all the losses since the conflict started in late February, with the currencies gauge also hitting a record high earlier in the week.

U.S. and Iranian officials were touting possible talks as early as this weekend. However, Iran flagged that the fate of its nuclear program was yet to be resolved and slow shipments through the Strait of Hormuz kept crude prices a hand's reach away from $100 a barrel.

Brazil's real slipped 0.2%, while the country's international bonds (BR018426994=), (105756CE8=) were steady.

The country said it raised a record 5 billion euros ($5.9 billion) with a bond sale on Wednesday, adding to the $4.5 billion sale of dollar-denominated bonds it issued in early February.

It would likely fuel volatility this year ahead of general elections in October. The country's benchmark interest rates are among the highest in the world at 14.75%.

Several Brazilian companies also announced bond sales including meatpacker Minerva <BEEF3.SA>, lender Banco do Brasil <BBAS3.SA>, according to IFR, and holding firm J&F Investimentos, according to newspaper Valor.

On the data front, the country's economic activity index, a proxy for gross domestic product, rose a seasonally adjusted 0.6% in February from the previous month, surpassing economists' expectations.

Elsewhere, the pesos of Chile and Mexico and Colombia were steady.

A 2% rise in crude prices lifted shares of Brazil's state-owned energy company, Petrobras by 2.5% and Colombia's Ecopetrol by 1.5%.

data.title: European Stocks Are Ready to Outperform the U.S. Here Are Three Reasons Why. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Kristina Hooper

About the author: Kristina Hooper is the chief market strategist at Man Group, a global alternative investment manager.

Market strategists and economists have written off Europe's economic potential and underweighted or overlooked its equities for decades. When investors compared Europe to the U.S., the latter looked more attractive. The U.S. had better exposure to technology and innovation, a more relaxed regulatory environment, and generally greater profitability.

There are signs that this status quo is changing and that investors are waking up to the opportunities offered by European equities. Europe outperformed U.S. equities in 2025, and European exchange-traded funds saw record inflows in the first two months of 2026. While U.S. stocks resumed their typical outperformance of European stocks when the Middle East war began, that trend is likely to stand only as long as the fighting lasts.

Recent paradigm shifts in U.S. and European trade, fiscal stimulus, innovation, and regulatory policies — as well as the outcome of the recent Hungarian election — suggest to me that European stocks are poised to resume its outperformance this year.

We are on the cusp of a period of European exceptionalism. Here are three reasons why.

Defense spending stimulus

Core European defense spending has doubled since 2019 and is expected to rise further, reaching 2.9% of gross domestic product — approximately 800 billion euros — by 2030.

The European Union is helping to support defense spending through the Security Action for Europe program, a EUR150 billion initiative to provide low-cost financing for member states through long-duration loans. Importantly, equipment purchased with SAFE loans must be mostly made in Europe.

The Hungarian presidential election results last week, in which Peter Magyar beat out the Russia-allied incumbent, could pave the way for greater EU alignment on foreign policy and defense spending. Magyar has already agreed to a proposed EUR90 billion defense loan from the EU to Ukraine, which Hungary had previously blocked. This substantial amount of fiscal stimulus has the potential to positively impact the broader European economy.

We are already seeing signs of improvement on the macro level. German defense spending has accelerated, and euro zone manufacturing activity has been on the rise in the last several months. This has translated into stock price moves: The STOXX Europe Aerospace & Defense ETF has returned 33.28% in the past year.

An American brain drain

Nearly a century ago, the U.S. benefited from a European brain drain, as scientists and academics were exiled or fled their home countries. Those immigrants helped enable the U.S. become the epicenter of innovation in the 20th century. We could now see a similar phenomenon unfold in Europe.

Restrictive U.S. immigration policies are making it difficult for international students to study at American universities, causing many to opt for Europe instead. For the 2025-2026 academic year, new enrollments of international students in the U.S. declined by 17%. The global higher education analysis firm Quacquarelli Symonds forecasts that international enrollment in European universities will grow by about 5% a year to 2030.

Under the second Trump administration, an estimated 95,000 federally employed science workers have been fired or left their jobs. Nearly 8,000 research grants worth billions of dollars have have been frozen or terminated nearly, prompting American academics and researchers to look for work elsewhere. Europe is luring them in with a variety of programs that incentivize emigration, such as the European Commission's EUR1.25 billion "Choose Europe for Science" initiative.

The European Research Council reported the number of U.S. applicants for its early-career grants nearly tripled, rising from 60 for the 2024 call to 169 for 2026. And in a recent survey of European start-ups, 40% said it had become somewhat or significantly easier to bring in top tech hires in 2025, compared to just 15% in 2021.

European regulatory reforms

Foreign investors historically preferred to work within the lighter regulatory framework of the U.S.; Europe's heavy regulatory regime made it a less appealing destination to do business. Most EU countries ranked below the U.S. in the World Competitiveness Rankings as recently as 2025.

The EU is trying to change that. It has started what it calls the "the Simplification Revolution" to make it easier to do business in Europe. This is part of the European Commission's commitment to reduce administrative burdens by 25% for all businesses and 35% for small and midsize enterprises by 2029.

At the same time, the half-century long laissez faire regulatory environment of the U.S. is ending. In the past several years, the U.S. has become far more involved in directing the private sector. The federal government has taken a golden share in U.S. Steel and a 10% equity stake in Intel. It has demanded Nvidia and AMD pay roughly 15% of their revenue from certain AI chip sales in China to the U.S. government as a condition for export licenses.

European equities still have low valuations compared to the U.S., meaning there is the potential for positive surprises this year. With relatively high-dividend yields, investors with exposure to European equities are being paid to wait.

Most importantly for the long term, we are seeing the EU lay the groundwork for a potential flourishing of capitalism by supporting technology and innovation and by reforming and deregulating where appropriate. For these reasons, I see attractive opportunities in European equities ahead.

Guest commentaries like this one are written by authors outside the Barron's newsroom. They reflect the perspective and opinions of the authors. Submit feedback and commentary pitches to ideas@barrons.com .

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Leftist Sanchez vies with far-right former mayor for spot in Peru's runoff vote
data.description:
By Marco Aquino

Leftist congressman Roberto Sanchez and far-right former Lima Mayor Rafael Lopez Aliaga were neck-to-neck on Thursday in the race to face conservative Keiko Fujimori in a June runoff vote for Peru's presidential election.

Sanchez held a less than 10,000-vote lead over Lopez Aliaga with 93% of ballots counted, as results continued to trickle in through Peru's ONPE electoral office five days after Sunday's vote, which was extended into Monday to accommodate several thousand people who were unable to vote.

Sanchez held 11.98% of votes compared to Lopez Aliaga's 11.92%, while Fujimori - the daughter of late former president Alberto Fujimori who is on her fourth run for the job - maintained a comfortable lead with 17.07%.

A second-round vote has been scheduled for June 7.

The extended vote count has triggered allegations of fraud, notably from Lopez Aliaga, who has called for counting to be suspended.

Lopez Aliaga's party has offered 20,000 soles (around $5,830) to electoral workers as a reward for information about "possible irregularities, fraud, or sabotage."

He has not produced evidence to back up his assertions, and European Union observers have said they found no concrete evidence of fraud.

Fujimori has called for calm.

"I have chosen not to comment until there is significant progress in the count," Fujimori said at a press conference on Wednesday night. "The result is going to be very close. Every vote will count, and the result is not yet decided."

Pre-election surveys suggested that a large number of voters were undecided or unwilling to vote for any candidate in the race, in which a record 34 candidates competed.

Markets have reacted nervously to Sanchez gaining ground.

He has pledged a new constitution and more state control over natural resources and is an ally of former President Pedro Castillo, who was ousted and jailed in 2022 after a short-lived presidency.

Peru, a major exporter of copper and agricultural goods, has faced years of political upheaval with eight presidents in the last decade. Several of its former leaders are behind bars.

Despite this, it has long been one of Latin America's most stable economies.

Alvaro Henzler, who heads Transparencia, an election-monitoring NGO, told local radio RPP that it may take a couple of weeks to get the full results, with most of the uncounted votes coming from broadly left-leaning rural areas and overseas, where emigrants tend to back right-wing parties.

"Transparencia is calling for calm and asking people to wait," he said.

data.title: Coty, Interparfums deny report of talks regarding Boss, Burberry perfume licenses
data.description:
Coty on Thursday said it is not in talks to sell any of its prestige brand licenses, denying a report by luxury news site Miss Tweed that the U.S. beauty company was discussing selling its Hugo Boss and Burberry licenses to Paris-based Interparfums .

"Any suggestion that Coty is in talks to transfer or sell prestige brand licences is categorically false and without merit," a Coty spokesperson said in a statement to Reuters.

Interparfums CEO Philippe Benacin also denied that any talks were taking place. "There are currently no discussions whatsoever underway," he said.

data.title: This Diagnostics Stock Is Sinking 30%. Why the Moderate Flu Season Was Bad for Business. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Kit Norton

Shares of QuidelOrtho tumbled on Thursday after the diagnostic testing company reported preliminary first-quarter revenue that came in well below Wall Street expectations.

QuidelOrtho currently expects preliminary unaudited first-quarter revenue between $615 million and $620 million, about 9% below Wall Street's estimate of $671.8 million, according to FactSet. The preliminary first-quarter sales numbers, reported late Wednesday, also represent a 11% decline from a year ago. Official earnings are expected to be reported on May 5.

The company blamed its preliminary numbers on a weaker respiratory illness season, citing a 30% drop in U.S. influenza-like illness visits compared with the first quarter of 2025.

The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention currently classifies the U.S. influenza season, which usually runs from October to May, as "moderate severity."

QuidelOrtho shares tumbled 30% to $12.25 on Thursday, on pace for their lowest closing price since April 25, 2011, according to Dow Jones Market Data. Shares are down 26% in April and have declined 58% so far this year.

QuidelOrtho added that slower China distributor sales, possibly because of the proposed China National Health Security Administration's reimbursement rate reductions, are also weighing on first-quarter sales. Some Europe, the Middle East, and Africa orders have also been delayed by the U.S. and Israel-led war with Iran, according to the company.

Based on these factors, the company continues to see free cash flow to be negative $65 million to $70 million in the first half of 2026. However, QuidelOrtho sees free cash flow turning positive for the full year.

"Despite macroeconomic challenges and a softer first quarter respiratory season, QuidelOrtho is taking decisive cost actions to drive full-year 2026 performance," CEO Brian J. Blaser said in the press release.

QuidelOrtho said it still believes the low end of its previous full-year 2026 guidance remains achievable. In February, the company forecast full-year earnings between $2.00 and $2.42 a share on sales of $2.7 billion to $2.9 billion.

Write to Kit Norton at kit.norton@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Treasury Reveals Details Of Twenty-Year Bond Auction
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - The Treasury Department on Thursday announced the details of this month's auction of twenty-year bonds.

The Treasury revealed plans to sell $13 billion worth of twenty-year bonds, with the results of the auction due to be announced next Wednesday.

Last month, the Treasury also sold $13 billion worth of twenty-year bond, attracting above average demand.

The Treasury announced the results of this month's auction of $58 billion worth of three-year notes, $39 billion worth of ten-year notes and $22 billion worth of thirty-year bonds last week.

The three-year note auction attracted above average demand, the ten-year note auction attracted below average demand and the thirty-year bond auction attracted average demand.

Copyright(c) 2026 RTTNews.com. All Rights Reserved

Copyright RTT News/dpa-AFX

data.title: Snap's activist fix is built to disappear
data.description:
By Sebastian Pellejero

Founder Evan Spiegel is largely ignoring demands from a pushy investor. Sure, he’s laying off 16% of workers at the $10 bln social media firm, but a risky smart glasses program continues. Complete voting control means he answers to nobody. The cost to shareholders is high.

Full view will be published shortly.

Follow Sebastian Pellejero on LinkedIn.

CONTEXT NEWS

Snap, the parent company of the messaging platform Snapchat, announced plans to slash up to 16% of its global workforce on April 15.
